Article Title: Two premier hospital RDAs seek President's intervention over Wangchuk case
Article Text:
Two Premier Hospital RDAs Seek President's Intervention Over Wangchuk Case (ANI images)

NEW DELHI: A day after the Resident Doctors' Association (RDA) of AIIMS wrote to President Droupadi Murmu over the Jantar Mantar crackdown and the continued hospitalisation of climate activist Sonam Wangchuk , the RDA of VMMC and Safdarjung Hospital on Tuesday also sought the President's intervention, raising concerns over patient autonomy, medical ethics, the independence of public healthcare institutions and the reported events at Jantar Mantar.In a four-page representation, the Safdarjung RDA said Wangchuk's medical condition and the need for continued hospitalisation should remain matters of independent clinical judgement, but every competent patient is entitled to dignity, informed consent and treatment in accordance with established medical ethics and the Constitution.The association said reports suggesting Wangchuk, despite being a competent patient, was allegedly not permitted to avail Leave Against Medical Advice (LAMA), if accurate, raise "serious concerns regarding informed consent, personal liberty and constitutional rights". Hospitals, it said, are institutions of healing and should not be perceived as places of detention except where restrictions are authorised by law and follow due process.The RDA also questioned the security arrangements at Safdarjung Hospital during Wangchuk's admission. Referring to reports and publicly available visuals, it said restricted access to hospital areas, extensive deployment of security personnel and disruption of routine hospital functioning, if established, warranted an independent examination.It also called for an independent, impartial and time-bound inquiry into the reported events at Jantar Mantar, citing reports and videos indicating the use of force, including lathi charge and tear gas, against peaceful protesters. The association further referred to allegations of intimidation and physical misconduct involving faculty members and students of Lady Hardinge Medical College, saying these too required an impartial investigation.The RDA said such incidents risk eroding public confidence in democratic and healthcare institutions and place healthcare professionals at greater risk of hostility despite having no role in administrative or political decisions. It added that hospital administrations must remain independent custodians of medical ethics and patient welfare and should never be perceived as aligned with non-medical or political interests.Describing its appeal as "not political", the association urged the President to order an independent inquiry into the recent events, safeguard competent patients' right to seek Leave Against Medical Advice wherever legally permissible, examine whether security arrangements at Safdarjung Hospital affected patient care and hospital functioning, ensure accountability if constitutional rights or medical ethics were violated, investigate allegations of excessive force against peaceful protesters and protect healthcare professionals from intimidation and violence.The Safdarjung RDA's representation comes a day after the AIIMS RDA sought the President's intervention over the Jantar Mantar incident and Wangchuk's continued hospitalisation, raising concerns over patient autonomy, constitutional rights and medical ethics. With Tuesday's letter, resident doctors from two of the country's premier government hospitals have now publicly sought intervention over the issue.The representation was signed by RDA President Dr Devaunshi Kaul, Vice-President Dr Rahul, General Secretary Dr Ayush Raj, Joint Secretary Dr Amit Yadav and Treasurer Dr Rohit Rathi.
Article Summary: Two premier hospital rdas seek president's intervention over wangchuk case. Resident doctors' association (rda) of aiims wrote to president droupadi murmu over the jantar mantar crackdown and the continued hospitalisation of sonam wangchuk. Rda of vmmc and safdarjung hospital on tuesday also sought the president's intervention.

Article Title: Bengal's Martyrs' Day turns into three-way battle for opposition space
Article Text:
The biggest event is being organised by the Mamata Banerjee-led faction of the Trinamool Congress near Birla Planetarium.

NEW DELHI: West Bengal's annual July 21 Martyrs' Day observance has turned into a major political showdown this year, with three rival groups set to hold separate programmes within a 2.5-km stretch in central Kolkata. What was once the Trinamool Congress' biggest annual political event has now become a battle over political legitimacy after the party split and lost power in the state assembly elections.The biggest event is being organised by the Mamata Banerjee-led faction of the Trinamool Congress near Birla Planetarium. A rival group led by leader of opposition Ritabrata Banerjee, which claims to be the "real Trinamool Congress", is holding its own rally near the Gandhi statue on Mayo Road. The Congress, which originally spearheaded the 1993 agitation, is also organising a separate programme at Shahid Minar.Hours before Tuesday's events, former chief minister Mamata Banerjee spent the night at her rally venue after her faction alleged that unidentified people had vandalised the stage, banners and sound system.Accompanied by senior party leaders including Abhishek Banerjee, Derek O'Brien, Kalyan Banerjee and Dola Sen, Mamata remained at the venue overnight while party workers guarded the site until morning.Addressing supporters shortly before midnight, Mamata accused the BJP of trying to disrupt the programme."Our workers are being stopped from coming to the meeting. Families of the martyrs are being intimidated by the police. Our stage has been vandalised, banners torn, and power and microphone lines cut. Around 60-65 BJP-backed goons, led by a criminal who is out on court orders, carried out the attack," she alleged.Without naming chief minister Suvendu Adhikari, she claimed the state government was using the police and intimidation to prevent people from attending the gathering. However, the BJP denied the allegations.State minister Dilip Ghosh questioned why Trinamool leaders had remained at the venue throughout the night."This is supposed to be a programme to honour martyrs, not a protest stage. They can sit there all night, but nobody is with them anymore. They betrayed the martyrs, their families and ultimately the people," Ghosh said.July 21 Martyrs' Day commemorates the killing of 13 Youth Congress workers who died in police firing during a march to Writers' Buildings in Kolkata in 1993. Since 1994, Mamata Banerjee has observed the anniversary every year, and it has grown into the Trinamool Congress' largest annual political event.This year's observance carries added political significance because it is the first since the BJP defeated the Trinamool Congress in the West Bengal Assembly elections, ending the party's 15-year rule. It is also the first Martyrs' Day after the Trinamool split into rival factions.Security has been tightened across central Kolkata as the three events are taking place close to one another. Police have set up barricades, diverted traffic, deployed drones and increased the number of personnel to prevent any clashes.The political atmosphere intensified further on Monday when chief minister Suvendu Adhikari tabled in the Assembly the long-pending judicial commission report into the 1993 police firing. He accused the previous Trinamool government of suppressing the report while continuing to use the incident for political purposes.The release of the report has added a new political dimension to this year's Martyrs' Day, with both the ruling BJP and rival Trinamool factions seeking to shape the legacy of an event that has remained one of West Bengal's most significant political anniversaries for more than three decades.
Article Summary: July 21 martyrs' day commemorates the killing of 13 youth congress workers who died in police firing during a march to writers' buildings in kolkata in 1993. Since 1994, mamata banerjee has observed the anniversary every year, and it has grown into the trinamool congress' largest annual political event.

Article Title: 'Pradhan has to be sacked': Sonam Wangchuk enters Day 24 of hunger strike
Article Text:
Activist Sonam Wangchuk

NEW DELHI: Scientist and activist Sonam Wangchuk on Tuesday entered the 24th consecutive day of his hunger strike, as the Cockroach Janta Party intensified its protest against the Centre over the NEET paper leak and reiterated its demand for Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation.CJP spokesperson Ashutosh Ranka said Wangchuk remained firm in his indefinite fast, which the group said was aimed at seeking accountability over the NEET fiasco.In a post on X, Ranka wrote: "Sonam Wangchuk is on 24th day of his hunger strike. He is still fighting for us. We won't stop. Pradhan has to be sacked."Apart from Wangchuk, other protesters also resumed their sit-in at Delhi's Jantar Mantar, continuing their agitation and pressing for Pradhan's resignation.CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke also continued the protest alongside party members at the demonstration site.A day earlier, the protesters march to Parliament drew a large crowd of supporters and was joined by several prominent personalities, including actors Shabana Azmi and Prakash Raj , as well as MPs Dimple Yadav and Chandrashekhar Azad.The protest, however, was marred by allegations of police excesses. The CJP accused the Delhi Police of using force against protesters, alleging that Sonam Wangchuk's wife, Gitanjali, along with Shabana Azmi and Prakash Raj, were among those targeted during the march.The Delhi Police, however, rejected the allegations, describing the claims circulating on social media as false and misleading.
Article Summary: Cjp spokesperson ashutosh ranka said wangchuk remained firm in his indefinite fast. Cockroach janta party reiterated its demand for union education minister dharmendra pradhan's resignation. Other protesters also resumed their sit-in at delhi's jantar mantar.

Article Title: Wangchuk continues to refuse IV fluids despite medical advice; low blood sugar, potassium persist
Article Text:
"Sonam Wangchuk being continuously monitored, kidney risk if fasting continues," said Safdarjung Hospital's statement.

NEW DELHI: Climate activist Sonam Wangchuk continues to decline intravenous fluids and glucose administration despite repeated medical advice, even as his blood sugar levels remained on the lower side, serum potassium dropped to 3.2 mEq/L and laboratory tests showed persistent pancytopenia, a condition marked by reduced blood cell counts, according to the latest health bulletin issued by VMMC & Safdarjung Hospital on Tuesday morning.The hospital said Wangchuk's vital parameters remain stable, but he continues to have persistent pancytopenia, characterised by anaemia and low leukocyte (white blood cell) counts. These clinical and laboratory findings warrant continued close monitoring through serial investigations, it said.Doctors said Wangchuk is currently receiving oral rehydration therapy (ORS) and oral potassium supplementation. However, he continues to refuse intravenous fluids and glucose administration despite repeated medical advice.According to the bulletin, Wangchuk remains under the close supervision of a multidisciplinary team of experts from VMMC & Safdarjung Hospital and AIIMS, New Delhi. In view of his ongoing mild to moderate dehydration, persistent laboratory abnormalities and the physiological stress associated with prolonged fasting, sustained medical supervision and continuous clinical monitoring remain essential for his recovery and for the timely identification and management of any potential complications, it said.The hospital said all necessary medical care is being provided and Wangchuk's clinical condition remains under close and continuous assessment. Further management will be guided by his clinical progress and serial laboratory investigations.
Article Summary: "sonam wangchuk being continuously monitored, kidney risk if fasting continues," said safdarjung hospital's statement. Wangchuk's vital parameters remain stable, but he continues to have persistent pancytopenia, characterised by anaemia and low leukocyte (white blood cell) counts.

Article Title: 'Truck carrying Abhijeet Dipke, Gitanjali, Shabana Azmi, Prakash Raj brutally attacked': CJP accuses Delhi Police of violence
Article Text:
CJP alleges police brutality during Parliament march

Ladenge jeetenge!

NEW DELHI: The Cockroach Janta Party (CJP), the pressure group leading the ongoing protest at Delhi's Jantar Mantar, on Tuesday levelled serious allegations of police brutality during its march to Parliament a day earlier, claiming that several protesters, including well-known public figures, were assaulted.The internet-sensation-turned-pressure group alleged that actors Prakash Raj and Shabana Azmi , along with Sonam Wangchuk 's wife Gitanjali and CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke, were inside a truck that was "brutally attacked" by the police.In a post on X, the CJP's official handle said, "CJP Founder Abhijeet Dipke, Gitanjali ji (Sonam sir’s wife), actors Shabana Azmi and Prakash Raj were all in this truck which the Police brutally attacked.They dragged one protester who was helping to navigate and chased him to beat him up. The Police thought we will get scared - but they are wrong!Adding to the allegations, CJP spokesperson Ashutosh Ranka claimed that Gitanjali, wife of educationist and activist Sonam Wangchuk, was dragged out of the vehicle by her hair during the police action.Recounting Monday's protest, Ranka said, "It was just the Police who used lathis. Our youths were beaten up, their heads cracked open, they were dragged on streets. Sonam sir's wife Gitanjali ma'am's hair was pulled in order to drag her out of truck...Ribs of one of our volunteers was shattered. We had never expected or seen such brutality. Delhi Police crossed all limits yesterday. Clearly, they were given instructions to beat up people.But this will further expand the protest. More number of people will come to Delhi. We will not move from here until Dharmendra Pradhan resigns."Ranka also expressed disappointment over the outcome of Monday's meeting with the government, saying, "We did not receive any response. 5 hours were wasted. If the Govt wants to talk to us, they will now have to come here."Meanwhile, the Delhi Police rejected the allegations, saying the claims made by the CJP were false and misleading.In a statement, the Delhi Police said, "False information is being currently circulated on social media regarding the posts claim that an individual named Geetanjali, a 12-year-old girl was assaulted, had her hair pulled, sustained a head injury, and approximately 40 to 50 other individuals suffered broken heads. This information is false. The claims being circulated in the posts are not true..."However, in a first formal engagement with the Centre since the protest began, CJP representatives met Union health minister Jagat Prakash Nadda on Monday.Following the meeting, CJP spokesperson Ashutosh Ranka claimed that Nadda had sought more time to consult the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) leadership on the group's demands before responding.
Article Summary: Cjp alleges police brutality during parliament march on monday. Delhi police reject the allegations, saying the claims made by the cjp are false and misleading. Cjp representatives met union health minister jagat prakash nadda on monday, but did not receive any response.

Article Title: Kerala merchant navy man dies in Russian strike off Ukraine ahead of wedding
Article Text:
Akhil Joyan (Picture from X)

NEW DELHI: A 26-year-old man from Kerala working in the merchant navy was killed after the cargo vessel he was serving on allegedly came under a Russian strike off the Ukrainian coast, people close to his family said on Monday.His death came just two months before he was due to get married.The deceased, Akhil Joyan, was the only son of Joyan and Sally, residents of Vellarikkundu in Kerala's Kasaragod district.Shobi Joseph, ward member of Vellarikkundu in Balal grama panchayat, told PTI that the family received an email from the shipping company informing them of Akhil's death on Monday evening."We are trying to gather more details and complete the necessary formalities. He joined the merchant navy at the age of 19," Joseph said.According to Joseph, Akhil had returned to his hometown around three months ago for his engagement ceremony."His marriage was scheduled to take place two months from now. The bride is from Alappuzha district. He returned to work a month back," he said.Joseph described Akhil as a well-known and respected figure in the locality, who was also a popular tug-of-war player."He was known as a well-behaved and disciplined youngster," he said.He added that Akhil's father worked at an electrical shop while his mother was an insurance agent."They are an ordinary family, and their future depended largely on Akhil. They had recently constructed a house," Joseph said.The family is now in touch with the local MP and MLA to seek assistance in bringing Akhil's body back to India and completing other formalities.According to foreign media reports, Ukraine's Sea Ports Authority said a cargo vessel flying the flag of Guinea-Bissau was hit by Russian forces on Sunday shortly after leaving the port of Odesa. The vessel, carrying Ukrainian corn, was reportedly struck while at sea. The authority said eight people were rescued from the ship.India strongly condemned the attack, saying that targeting commercial shipping, endangering the lives of civilian crew members, or impeding freedom of navigation and commerce is deplorable and must be avoided.
Article Summary: Akhil joyan, 26, was killed after the cargo vessel he was serving on allegedly came under a russian strike off the ukrainian coast. His death came just two months before he was due to get married. India strongly condemned the attack, saying that targeting commercial shipping, endangering the lives of civilian crew members is deplorable.

Article Title: Sonam Wangchuk's wife challenges Delhi HC order, says it 'illegally confines' him to Safdarjung hospital
Article Text:
Poll Vote & Share your view What is your opinion on medical intervention without patient consent? It can be necessary in emergencies It can violate patient rights Depends on the situation 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

A day after the Delhi high court declined to direct the transfer of climate activist Sonam Wangchuk from Safdarjung Hospital to a private hospital, his wife Dr Gitanjali J Angmo on Monday challenged the order before a Division Bench, alleging that it "illegally confines" him to the government hospital despite him not being under arrest.According to PTI, Angmo argued that "Sunday's order effectively directs that neither Wangchuk nor his wife have decisive authority in determining medical treatment."She further told the court, "Sunday order illegally confines Sonam Wangchuk to Safdarjung hospital without arrest."This came after Wangchuk's wife sought an urgent hearing before the Division Bench challenging the Single Judge's order on Sunday, contending that it violates Wangchuk's fundamental rights to bodily autonomy, informed consent and personal liberty.In her appeal, Angmo argued that the Single Judge's order effectively takes away the authority of Wangchuk and his family to make decisions regarding his medical treatment by leaving the final decision to the doctors at Safdarjung Hospital.The plea states that the order does not adequately address the issue of informed consent and ignores the settled legal principle that a competent patient has the right to accept, refuse or discontinue medical treatment.It further argues that although the Single Judge recorded that Wangchuk is neither under arrest nor detention, the order effectively confines him to Safdarjung Hospital without any legal authority.The appeal alleges that the order authorises medical intervention, including the administration of food, without Wangchuk's consent, thereby violating his bodily autonomy under Article 21 of the Constitution.It also contends that his continued stay at Safdarjung Hospital infringes his rights under Articles 19 and 21, as it prevents him from continuing his peaceful hunger strike and protest at Jantar Mantar.According to the appeal, Wangchuk was never consulted before being shifted to Safdarjung Hospital, and neither he nor his wife was given the opportunity to choose the hospital or the doctors treating him.The plea further argues that there was no evidence of any deterioration in his health warranting such coercive action. It says that if the authorities believed immediate medical intervention was necessary, they should have sought the court's permission before removing him from the protest site.The appeal seeks to set aside the July 19 order and requests directions allowing Wangchuk to be shifted to a private hospital of his choice while safeguarding his rights relating to medical treatment and personal liberty.On Saturday, Justice Mini Pushkarna refused to grant interim relief, observing that there was no prima facie violation of Wangchuk's personal liberty, as the government's decision to shift him from the protest site was taken to protect his life after his health deteriorated during a 17-18 day fast.The court noted that the transfer to Safdarjung Hospital was carried out in compliance with a Division Bench order dated July 16 directing daily medical monitoring and necessary medical intervention to prevent further deterioration in his condition.The High Court also observed that doctors were administering only those medicines and oral electrolytes to which Wangchuk had consented and found nothing to suggest that force was being used against him or that his bodily autonomy had been violated.It further recorded the Centre's submission that Wangchuk's wife, brother and brother-in-law had been granted unrestricted 24x7 access to meet him and that a separate room had been provided for the family. The court also noted the Centre's assurance that Wangchuk's medical reports would be shared with his family.Holding that no case for interim relief was made out, the high court directed the Union government to file a status report within three days and listed the matter for further hearing on July 24.
Article Summary: Sonam wangchuk's wife dr gitanjali j angmo on monday challenged the order before a division bench. She alleges that it "illegally confines" him to the government hospital despite him not being under arrest. On saturday, the delhi high court declined to direct the transfer of wangchuk from safdarjung hospital to a private hospital.

Article Title: Rajasthan horror: 7-year-old girl raped in Barmer on pretext of showing house; neighbour arrested
Article Text: JAISALMER: A 7-year-old girl was allegedly raped by her neighbour in Rajasthan's Barmer city on Sunday evening after he allegedly lured her away on the pretext of showing a house and took her to a secluded area, where the crime was reportedly committed in the bushes.Police have arrested the accused.According to police, the rape incident involving a 7-year-old girl was reported on Sunday evening in an area of Barmer city. Acting swiftly, police conducted an on-site inspection. The accused has been taken into custody.The minor was playing in front of her house when the accused youth mentioned the name of an unknown person and asked her to show his house. He took her along with him. He then lured her to a deserted place and raped her there.Crying in pain, the child reached home and narrated the ordeal to her family members.The family immediately took the child to the hospital. Upon receiving information, police reached the spot and recorded the family's statement. A team also inspected the crime scene and visited the girl’s house to gather complete details.Police have detained the suspect, Magh Singh, son of Tej Singh. Following the incident, anger erupted among family members and locals, who expressed outrage against the accused. Police have got the girl’s medical examination done.Barmer ASP Nitesh Arya said, "The accused has been arrested. An SIT has been constituted. The minor’s medical examination is being conducted. The matter is under investigation."
Article Summary: A 7-year-old girl was allegedly raped by her neighbour in rajasthan's barmer city. Magh singh allegedly lured her away on the pretext of showing a house. He then took her to a secluded area, where the crime was reportedly committed in the bushes.

Article Title: NEET paper leak: No need for Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation, action taken against culprits, says Ramdas Athawale
Article Text:
Ramdas Athawale, Dharmendra Pradhan

SRINAGAR: Union Minister Ramdas Athawale on Saturday said his colleague, Dharmendra Pradhan , does not need to resign over the NEET paper leak controversy, asserting that action has already been taken against those involved in the scam.Athawale also urged activist Sonam Wangchuk to call off his hunger strike, saying the government would take steps to ensure such paper leaks do not occur in the future, according to news agency PTI."There is no need for Dharmendra Pradhan to resign. NEET paper leak is not a good thing for us, but it is not as if no action has been taken in this regard," Athawale told reporters on the sidelines of an event here.The Union minister said several accused have been arrested in connection with the paper leak case, including a significant number from Maharashtra."Wangchuk has the right to give suggestions on how to prevent paper leak, but there is no need for Pradhan to resign," he added.
Article Summary: Ramdas athawale says there is no need for dharmendra pradhan to resign. He also urges activist sonam wangchuk to call off his hunger strike. Several accused have been arrested in connection with the paper leak case.

Article Title: 70 detained, 118 cops and 60 marchers injured: Police block protesters' march to Parliament; JP Nadda meets CJP representatives
Article Text:
Protesters clash with police during Cockroach Janta Party's march towards Parliament in New Delhi as security personnel use barricades to stop the demonstration (Image credit: PTI)

NEW DELHI: Cockroach Janta Party's march towards Parliament Monday to demand the resignation of Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan devolved into chaos as thousands of protesters ran into police barricades, lathis, and tear gas. While CJP alleged excesses by cops as protesters breached barricades and marched ahead, police said the agitators resorted to violence. By the end of the day, police said 118 of their personnel and 60 protesters were injured.Seventy people were detained, and cops said an FIR is being filed on charges of rioting, assault on public servants, and damage to public property.Many protesters had gathered at Jantar Mantar overnight. By 11am, crowds started to fill up adjoining roads as well. Traffic in central Delhi went haywire as the clashes spread beyond the protest site.Though CJP representatives met JP Nadda, CJP asked protesters to return to Jantar Mantar, where the stage was dismantled hours before.Meanwhile, BJP Monday blamed Left-affiliated student organisations, such as All India Students' Association (AISA) and Students' Federation of India (SFI), besides organised groups for protests and violence during Cockroach Janata Party's march to Parliament.Similar allegations of organised instigation and crowd mobilisation were made in connection with the Delhi riots, and several individuals continue to face legal proceedings in those cases, BJP said. The violence did not appear spontaneous and there were organised groups that initiated it, after which sections of the crowd followed, it alleged.In a hard-hitting statement, BJP functionary Amit Malviya said that the "stone-pelting and orchestrated violence" carried out in the name of students raise serious questions about the intent of the protestors and those backing them."Even more telling was the conspicuous absence of many of the prominent faces associated with the protest from the march itself, leaving those who had been mobilised at the behest of their political patrons to face the consequences," he asked. The investigation will establish criminal responsibility, Malviya said."The real conspirators are often those who plan, encourage and coordinate such violence while carefully keeping themselves out of harm's way. They move on to the next agitation, leaving those they mobilised to bear the legal consequences."CJP had been holding protest at Jantar Mantar since last month and has drawn support from opposition parties over its demand for the resignation of Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan for paper leaks.With CJP seeking to mobilise youngsters over alleged irregularities in exams, Malviya rejected some of the allegations floating around. "Facts matter. Narratives built on fabricated documents do not," he said.
Article Summary: Cockroach janta party's march towards parliament devolved into chaos as thousands of protesters ran into police barricades, lathis, and tear gas. By the end of the day, police said 118 of their personnel and 60 protesters were injured. Cjp alleged excesses by cops as protesters breached barricades and marched ahead, while police said the agitators resorted to violence.

Article Title: J&K floods: Lal Chowk flooded, Amarnath yatra stays suspended
Article Text: SRINAGAR: A heavy spell of rainfall Monday morning inundated Srinagar’s Lal Chowk, the city’s iconic square that has emerged as a major tourist attraction since the abrogation of Article 370.Images of a flooded Lal Chowk and the clock tower were widely circulated on social media, with netizens mocking the govt over the alleged failure of the drainage system in the city.Several other parts of Srinagar also witnessed waterlogging, with many questioning Srinagar’s Smart City infrastructure project.Officials of the Srinagar Municipal Corporation (SMC) said the capital city’s drainage system was overwhelmed by the intensity of the rainfall. “It was a heavy downpour for nearly two hours,” an official said, adding that dewatering pumps were deployed to clear waterlogged areas.The explanation, though, was shot down by many.“The idea of a Smart City is to employ technologies, data and intelligent systems to improve efficiency, sustainability, quality of life and resource management with the approval of the citizens. Smart City Srinagar was based on contextless, soulless and photoshopped images,” said Hakim Sameer Hamdani, a conservation architect.“We need to have a way to manage run-off water from the road and ideally that should be treated and used to recharge groundwater. But there is no proper and integrated system,” Hamdani said.SMC officials, however, said all works sanctioned under the Srinagar Smart City project, which began in April 2017, were completed.Last month, the Omar Abdullah cabinet raised concerns about the award of contracts and the implementation of projects under the Smart City initiative. His govt ordered an external audit on the “failure of the Srinagar Smart City” project.On Monday, the CM said a brief spell of rain flooded large parts of the city, including Lal Chowk, indicating that the drainage system failed. “They are saying they have completed the smart city project. Our govt wants to know what has been achieved so far,” he said.Last year, the ministry of housing and urban affairs told the Lok Sabha that under the Union territory’s Smart Cities portfolio, Srinagar was to complete 161 projects at Rs 3,634 crore.Meanwhile, the IMD Director Mukhtar Ahmad said that widespread rain will continue across Kashmir for the next three days with a risk of landslides and flash floods, asking people to remain vigilant and avoid venturing near hill slopes, rivers and streams.Lieutenant Governor Shri Manoj Sinha reviewed the situation following heavy rains, flash floods and landslides in several districts, mostly in Rajouri and Poonch districts, as death toll climbed to 17 people with several still missing due to flash floods.The govt has already extended school holidays up to July 23 and Shri Amarnath Yatra has been temporarily suspended from July 19, both from Baltal and Pahalgam routes.“I spoke to senior officials today to assess the impact of heavy rains, flash floods and landslides. I am deeply saddened by the loss of two lives in Doda, and my heart goes out to their families. Emergency teams, including NDRF, SDRF, J&K Police and the Army, are deployed and on high alert in vulnerable areas. Key roads in Poonch, Rajouri, Reasi and Udhampur have been reopened, and work to restore water supplies in several areas is ongoing. Field teams have also been directed to urgently evaluate damage to houses and public infrastructure,” he said.
Article Summary: Heavy rain inundates srinagar's iconic lal chowk. Officials say drainage system was overwhelmed by the intensity of the rainfall. Many question the city's smart city infrastructure project. Imd says widespread rain will continue across kashmir for the next three days.

Article Title: Supreme Court orders new SIT into Ram temple donation theft, will monitor probe
Article Text:
Don’t play politics over donation chori, says SC



NEW DELHI: Taking over supervision of Ayodhya Ram Mandir fund embezzlement scam investigations, the Supreme Court on Monday ousted local police from probing the charges by entrusting it to a SIT led by a senior IPS officer and directed Shri Ram Teerth Kshetra Trust to preserve receipts of donations it received since commencement of construction of the temple.Skeptical about sudden flurry of petitions seeking various means and mechanism for investigations into the donation pilferage charges, a bench of CJI Surya Kant, and Justices Joymalya Bagchi and V Mohana said, “We are putting a word of caution - do not play politics as courts are not meant for it. Do not politicise the issue.”The bench added, “It is a case of commission of offence under penal laws which must be investigated in a fair, impartial and transparent manner, and it should be taken to a logical conclusion.”SG Tushar Mehta, who submitted a sealed cover report on status of probe by UP police after a three-member SIT ordered registration of FIR on concluding that embezzlement of funds has taken place, said eight persons have been arrested and that the UP govt is ready to abide by whatever direction the SC passes for an independent and impartial probe into the allegations.The CJI said, “Sometimes we have to be practical. If the relief you are seeking is ordered, then people will claim they had donated diamonds and allege these are missing.”However, the bench said the trust must maintain all receipts of donations and accounts to facilitate scrutiny by the to-be-constituted SIT.Sceptical about the sudden flurry of petitions seeking various means and mechanism for investigations into the donation theft charges, a bench of CJI Surya Kant, and Justices Joymalya Bagchi and V Mohana said, “We are putting (in) a word of caution — do not play politics as courts are not meant for it. Do not politicise the issue. It is a case of commission of offence under penal laws which must be investigated in a fair, impartial and transparent manner, and it should be taken to a logical conclusion. ”The solicitor general, who submitted a sealed cover report on the status of the probe by UP police after a three-member SIT ordered registration of an FIR on concluding that embezzlement of funds has taken place, said eight persons were arrested.Unwilling to leave the probe in the hands of local police given the traction the issue has received among citizens, the bench told UP govt that it must constitute an SIT headed by a senior IPS officer and comprising other police officers selected by the DGP who should periodically report to SC about the progress in the investigation.The CJI-led bench said the SIT must include Kiran S, the IG (Lucknow Range), who was part of the three-member SIT set up by state govt that had inquired into the charges to find out whether embezzlement had been committed. On the earlier SIT’s recommendation, the FIR was registered. “Meanwhile, we will go through the probe status report and pass additional directions if needed,” it said and posted the matter for hearing on July 27.For a PIL petitioner, advocate Devadatt Kamat said the trust had collected huge amounts through donations from millions of devotees for temple construction and requested the court to direct the temple trust to upload the receipts of such donation in its website.“There are many who have donated gold and silver ornaments which are allegedly missing. An inventory of gold and silver ornaments or bricks must be prepared by the trust and uploaded on the website,” he said.The CJI said, “Sometimes we have to be practical. If the relief you are seeking is ordered, then people will claim they had donated diamonds and allege these are missing.” However, the bench said the trust must maintain all receipts of donations and accounts to facilitate scrutiny by the to-be-constituted SIT.
Article Summary: Supreme court orders shri ram teerth kshetra trust to preserve receipts of donations it received since commencement of construction of the temple. Cji surya kant, and justices joymalya bagchi and v mohana said, “we are putting a word of caution - do not play politics as courts are not meant for it.”

Article Title: 'We made govt bow and listen to us': CJP after meeting Nadda; protest continues at Jantar Mantar
Article Text:
Protest continues at Jantar Mantar

Here are the top developments:

Sonam continues fast, protest continues at Jantar Mantar

CJP meets Nadda



Delhi Police personnel injured



CJP’s three demands







Amit Shah meets Dharmendra Pradhan

Five metro stations shut, commuters stranded

Seven CJP protesters detained near RBI, protests in Gujarat

CJP Protesters reach near RBI, raise slogans

Tear Gas fired near Shastri Bhawan amid protest

Security tightened ahead of Monsoon Session

Dipke ends hunger strike as Centre opens dialogue with CJP

Police baton-charge protesters near Parliament Street, several detained

'Lathi-charge at Jantar Mantar': Kharge alleges during Monsoon Session of Parliament

Delhi Police refutes reports of violence

AAP leaders reach at Jantar Mantar

CJP activists say ready for dialogue with government, await clarity

Police use mild force to disperse CJP protesters

NEW DELHI: Tensions remained high in the national capital on Monday as the Cockroach Janata Party's (CJP) 'Sansad Chalo' march turned confrontational, with police using tear gas and baton charges after protesters allegedly attempted to breach barricades en route to Parliament. More than 50 Delhi Police personnel were injured in the clashes, with police alleging that protesters pelted stones, while the CJP accused the force of using excessive force against demonstrators.Even as the Centre opened a channel for talks, activist Sonam Wangchuk said he would continue his hunger strike until protesters were allowed to meet parliamentarians or he was permitted to meet them from the hospital. CJP leaders later met Union minister JP Nadda and reiterated three key demands, including the resignation of Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan, while maintaining that the agitation at Jantar Mantar would continue until their demands were met.Sonam Wangchuk said that he would continue the hunger-strike until the protesters were allowed to meet the Parliamentarians."I will continue my fast. Seeing the brutality with which peacefully protesting Youth are being dealt with, I have decided to continue my fast untill the youth leaders are allowed to meet the Parliamentarians at the Sansad Bhawan or l am allowed to meet them here at the Hospital.Hopefully, the government will fix the accountability of the Education Minister before then. I am touched and moved by how the youth have held on to peace despite provocation," he said.CJP said that the protest at Jantar Mantar continues until Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan resigned."The cockroaches have achieved a major victory today! We made the Government bow down and listen to us. We will not stop till Dharmendra Pradhan resigns," spokesperson Ashutosh Ranka said in a post on X.Moreover, CJP founder Abhijit Dipke briefly fainted during sit-in at Jantar Mantar.The CJP spokespersons Saurav Das and Ashutosh Ranka held a meeting with the Union minister JP Nadda, however, no commitments have been made so far, the spokesperson informed."Ashutosh Ranka and I have been at JP Nadda’s residence since 12 noon. The demands have been conveyed, including immediate resignation/sacking of Dharmendra Pradhan. The Minister assured us he will discuss this at the appropriate level. However, no commitments have been made so far. The peaceful protesters will not rest until the demand is met!" Saurav Das said in a post on X.Nadda urged the protesters to end their sit-in and "assist the administration in restoring normalcy.""This morning, for the first time, a proposal came from the protesters to hold talks with the government, and our discussions have been ongoing since 11:50am. The meeting took place in a cordial atmosphere. An initial verbal discussion was held in detail with their delegation, and they submitted a written petition to me around 4pm. I have requested all the protesters to end their sit-in and assist the administration in restoring normalcy," he said.Delhi Police personnel were injured during clashes with protesters taking part in the CJP's Parliament march, with police alleging that stones were pelted at security personnel during the confrontation."We were standing near barricades, stone was pelted by them (protesters)," ANI quoted cop saying.The CJP, however, accused the police of using excessive force against demonstrators, alleging that protesters, including Wangchuk's wife and a 12-year-old girl, were assaulted during the crackdown. The exchange of allegations came after tensions escalated as protesters attempted to breach police barricades near Parliament.The Delhi Police rejected these claims saying: "False information is being currently circulated on social media regarding the posts claim that an individual named Geetanjali, a 12-year-old girl was assaulted, had her hair pulled, sustained a head injury, and approximately 40 to 50 other individuals suffered broken heads. This information is false. The claims being circulated in the posts are not true. Please do not spread or amplify false or misleading information. "Meanwhile, SP MP Akhilesh Yadav slammed the Centre over police action against protesters in Delhi, alleging that students and youth were subjected to lathi charges, tear gas and rough treatment by personnel in plainclothes.Claiming the government had "failed" to anticipate the scale of the protests, Yadav said the anger among young people reflected years of frustration and asserted that the opposition would raise the issue in the Lok Sabha on Tuesday. He also demanded that the government clarify its stand on the alleged Ram Mandir donation controversy."We witnessed the conduct of the government and the police, the use of lathis and tear gas, and the way police personnel in plainclothes treated the students and youth...The government did not anticipate that students and the youth would turn out in such large numbers. This represents a massive failure, and it is precisely because of this failure that people have gathered here...For years, the government has been playing with the future of students and the youth," he said."That very anger is visible now; the call for revolution is rising, and a sense of solidarity is evident. That is why the country's youth are present here in such vast numbers. We will raise the incident that occurred today in the Lok Sabha tomorrow...The government must clarify its stance regarding the Ram Mandir donation controversy. The theft of offerings, donations, and contributions is shameful; it is a grave sin. Who will answer for this grave sin? The government must step forward to address it," he added.Around 70 protesters have been detained and FIR has been registered under relevant sections, PTI reported citing Delhi Police.CJP leaders Ashutosh Ranka and Saurav Das said they raised three key demands with Union Minister J.P. Nadda, including Sonam Wangchuk’s release, Dharmendra Pradhan’s resignation, and ₹1 crore compensation for families of NEET aspirants who died by suicide.Union home minister Amit Shah met education minister Dharmendra Pradhan amid the CJP’s march demanding Pradhan’s resignation over alleged examination irregularities. Officials said the meeting took place at Pradhan’s office in the Parliament complex for nearly four hours, though details of the discussion were not disclosed.The CJP has been protesting at Jantar Mantar since June 20, seeking exam reforms and action over alleged NEET irregularities.The Delhi Metro Rail Corporation (DMRC) closed five metro stations on Monday due to security concerns over the CJP’s Parliament march, causing chaos for hundreds of commuters. Crowds gathered at Rajiv Chowk, Patel Chowk and other stations as gates remained shut. Some protesters assembled inside metro premises and raised slogans, while security personnel used mild force to disperse people near Patel Chowk station.At least seven Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) protesters were detained near the Reserve Bank of India, close to Parliament, during the party’s ‘Sansad Chalo’ march in New Delhi, PTI reported.The protests were held in several cities across Gujarat on Monday in support of the CJP’s campaign against alleged irregularities in the education sector and exam paper leaks, as well as in solidarity with activist Sonam Wangchuk.In Ahmedabad, police detained several people who gathered at Law Garden for the demonstration, citing lack of permission for the protest, officials said. Security remained tightened in Delhi as CJP supporters attempted to march towards Parliament.CJP protesters reached near the Reserve Bank of India, close to Parliament, and raised slogans as security forces maintained a tight vigil in the area, PTI reported.Security forces fired tear gas shells near Shastri Bhawan after protesters allegedly tried to breach barricades and march towards Jantar Mantar to join the Cockroach Janta Party's Parliament march, sources said. Security was tightened across the high-security zone, with heavy deployment of police and paramilitary personnel. Access to nearby metro stations was restricted, several gates were closed, and commuters were stranded as authorities took precautionary measures.Security was intensified near Parliament premises, with Rapid Action Force personnel deployed to manage protesters and other contingencies. Several police officials were reportedly injured during clashes, while authorities maintained a heavy security presence across central Delhi.Also Read: 'They came very late': CJP claims govt reached out, but 'no clarity' on talks As the Cockroach Janata Party's (CJP) protest against the Centre intensified, founder Abhijeet Dipke ended his indefinite hunger strike at the request of activist Sonam Wangchuk, who is undergoing treatment at Safdarjung Hospital after a 21-day fast. Meanwhile, CJP representatives Saurav Das and Ashutosh Ranka left to meet Union Health Minister J P Nadda after the government reached out for talks. Thousands of supporters continued to gather at Jantar Mantar, pressing for examination reforms and action over the alleged NEET-UG 2026 paper leak.Police baton-charged protesters participating in the Cockroach Janata Party's (CJP) 'Sansad Chalo' march near Parliament Street on Monday, with several demonstrators detained, according to news agency PTI.During the Monsoon Session of Parliament on Monday, Leader of the opposition in the Rajya Sabha Mallikarjun Kharge raised the alleged NEET paper leak issue and claimed that a lathi-charge had taken place at the student protest at Jantar Mantar.Addressing the House, Kharge said, "This is a matter concerning students; I am speaking about the futures of lakhs of children. Thousands of students have gathered at Jantar Mantar for this cause. A lathi-charge has taken place there. The government is trying to use force, suppress voices, and drive them away."Amid social media reports of tensions at Jantar Mantar, Delhi Police on Monday dismissed claims of violence during the Cockroach Janata Party (CJP) protest, saying reports of "sporadic use of violence/detentions" were misleading.Senior Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) leaders, including Manish Sisodia, Sanjay Singh and Atishi, arrived at Jantar Mantar to participate in the Parliament march. Earlier Manish Sisodia had appealed to people across political affiliations to join the proposed march to Parliament, urging citizens to stand in solidarity with the country's youth.CJP activists said they are open to holding talks with the government, while maintaining that they are awaiting clarity from the authorities on the proposed dialogue.Speaking to ANI, CJP activist Saurav Das said, “The administration is trying to open talks with the government. They have come very late, but we have opened our hearts and said that we are willing to talk to them. There has been no clarity from their side as of now. The ball is in their court.”Delhi Police used mild force on Monday to disperse CJP protesters attempting to march towards Parliament House from Jantar Mantar. Security personnel intervened as hundreds of demonstrators moved ahead, causing traffic disruptions. Rapid Action Force personnel were deployed to maintain order and manage the situation during the Parliament session.
Article Summary: Protest continues at jantar mantar as centre opens channel for talks. Activist sonam wangchuk says he will continue his hunger strike until protesters are allowed to meet parliamentarians. Cjp leaders meet union minister jp nadda and reiterated three key demands.

Article Title: Day 1 of Parliament’s monsoon session washed out amid NEET, donation chori protests
Article Text:
‘No decision on ethanol blending beyond 20%’



NEW DELHI: The first day of Parliament’s monsoon session was a washout as opposition parties protested over a host of issues, including theft of donations at Ayodhya Ram temple and NEET paper leak, leading to frequent adjournments in Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha before proceedings were called off for the day.Amid the brief moments for which LS sat between several adjournments, law minister Arjun Ram Meghwal introduced a bill to replace an ordinance, which has increased the number of Supreme Court judges from 34 to 38, including the Chief Justice of India.In RS, leader of the opposition Mallikarjun Kharge raised the issue of NEET paper leak, which he called a scam, affecting the future of lakhs of students. “Today thousands of students have gathered at Jantar Mantar. There has been a lathi charge, and govt is trying to stifle dissent,” drawing protests from treasury benches and prompting Chairman C P Radhakrishnan to adjourn the House for half an hour.The protests, however, could not obscure the changed arithmetic. With rebel TMC MPs in LS being allotted different seats after their entry in the BJP-led NDA, what stood out was the front-row seat given to their head Sudip Bandyopadhyay.He occupied the seat of TMC parliamentary party leader Abhishek Banerjee, who now commands a group of eight MPs from earlier 28 and no longer has a front seat.It could have been the distinguishing feature of the opening day but for the protests, which continued in the two Houses. Despite appeals from LS Speaker Om Birla and RS Chairman Radhakrishnan for order, protests continued in the two Houses.Birla chided some opposition members for raising slogans when obituary references were read out on the demise of former MPs, saying it was not a good precedent. He later asked protesting opposition members to maintain order and called for allowing other members to participate in parliamentary proceedings, but in vain. The House reassembled six times before being adjourned for the day.RS was adjourned five times during the day. Earlier, soon after the recently elected MPs took oath, Radhakrishan invoked the “collective responsibility” of members to utilise time to debate and decide on issues of national importance. While urging members to remain focused on the timely disposal of business, he said every disruption delays discussion on matters affecting the nation and the people they represent.Amid concerns over E20 petrol, govt Monday said no decision had been taken to increase ethanol blending in petrol beyond 20%, reports Atul Mathur.Govt informed Parliament there was no proposal to make lower-blended fuel or pure petrol available at select retail outlets. It ruled out any immediate move to introduce ethanol blending in diesel.“Any move to increase blending in petrol beyond 20% would be based on scientific and technical studies and consultations with stakeholders,” minister of state for petroleum and natural gas Suresh Gopi told Rajya Sabha.
Article Summary: Opposition parties protested over a host of issues, including theft of donations at ayodhya ram temple and neet paper leak. Law minister arjun ram meghwal introduced bill to replace an ordinance, which has increased the number of supreme court judges from 34 to 38.

Article Title: Supreme Court takes exception to meagre compensation for ex-HC judges
Article Text: NEW DELHI: Supreme Court Monday took exception to the meagre monetary compensation provided to retired chief justices and judges of high courts, which varies vastly from one state to another, and asked the Union govt to constitute a committee to lay down uniform pan-India guidelines.A bench of CJI Surya Kant and Justices Joymalya Bagchi and V Mohana took exception to the UP govt’s challenge to an interim order of Allahabad HC directing the state to provide security even though it is paying Rs 65,000 and Rs 60,000 to retired CJs and judges of the HC towards security, a driver, domestic help, secretarial services, and telephone and internet expenses.The bench said HC judges, while holding constitutional posts, decide sensitive matters and their apprehensions about personal security are genuine. “Post-retirement, the judges need basic minimum facilities to lead a dignified life. Can Rs 60,000 provide all these services? We all know how much one needs to pay to employ a driver and a domestic help,” the CJI-led bench said.Finding solicitor general Tushar Mehta in the courtroom, the bench asked him to impress upon Centre to constitute a committee in two weeks to consider what should be the basic minimum security and services that every state must provide to retired HC CJs and judges to enable them to lead a dignified life.“We are not talking of a luxurious life, but a dignified life,” the CJI said.Mehta said such a panel would be constituted within two weeks as per request of the Supreme Court. The bench said the committee will study the issues faced by retired HC judges and give its recommendations within three months laying down basic guidelines on the obligations of the states to extend facilities to judges post-retirement.
Article Summary: Supreme court asks union govt to constitute a committee to lay down uniform pan-india guidelines. A bench of cji surya kant and justices joymalya bagchi and v mohana took exception to the up govt’s challenge to an interim order of allahabad hc.

Article Title: Who will ask if you’re home? The economy growing around urban loneliness
Article Text:
From joint family to flat

The silver economy

The business of being heard

The small economies of a lonely weekend

Who will pay for connection?

India is the second-loneliest country in the world, according to a study by JB.com. The ranking offers a stark lens through which to view a country that is also aging rapidly, urbanising quickly and watching its traditional social structures thin out.In 2011, India had about 100 million people aged 60 and above. Projections by the Technical Group on Population Projections, cited in the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation’s report Elderly in India 2021, show that this figure will more than double to roughly 230 million by 2036, around 15 per cent of the population. By 2050, UNFPA’s India Ageing Report 2023 estimates, nearly one in five Indians will be 60 or older.But loneliness is not waiting for old age.It is already present in the rented flats of young migrants, in the quiet of a single-person home, in weekends spent scrolling through other people's lives and in the small, almost invisible moments when there is no one to ask, “Are you home?”For Minal, from Bihar and living alone in Pune, loneliness is “feeling I don’t belong anywhere and nobody cares about me.” She calls herself “pretty social”, but in her current city “has only few people I know so I have phases when I go out alone.”A typical weekend is split between “one day I go out to eat somewhere nice and one day I stay in, cook something and journal or watch a movie.”At home, she spends “8–10 hours” on screens. After closing Instagram and seeing peers travelling or getting engaged, she says she feels “empty and restless and also shit about myself”.Swadha, who spent years living alone in Noida for college and work, remembers the strange contradiction of returning home.“There is a sense of relief… but then there is also a sense of silence and nobody is there to ask are you home… nobody cares whether you entered with the shoe on or have you left the shoe outside.”She placed a mirror at the entrance of her home. “Somebody has to look at me, right? I used to do that for myself.”Living alone, she says, “means you have absolutely no one to talk to about your day… that will hit you so hard that it will push you away from your loved ones whom you actually speak to on the calls.”For Neha, from West Bengal and working in Delhi, loneliness is even simpler: “no person to go back to when you feel heavy… no one to ask where you are… how have you been.”She says she has “no social life”. Her weekends are spent “grocery shopping… cooking and cleaning…”. When she returns home, her first thought is, “what should I eat now.” She estimates her screen time at 4–5 hours a day at home and says that after closing social apps, she feels, “I’m wasting my time… I should do something productive.”Their lives may seem far removed from the concerns of India's elderly. But the emotional patterns are strikingly similar.A LASI-based study on later life reports that about 13.4 per cent of older Indians experience frequent loneliness. The odds are higher among those not in a marital union, those with more than two chronic conditions and those with small social networks or limited leisure activities. UNFPA’s 2023 report identifies social isolation and loneliness as key stressors on elderly mental health, aggravated by migration, urbanisation and weakening family support.The country is aging into this crisis.India's elderly population is not evenly distributed. Kerala's elderly share is projected to rise from about 13 per cent in 2011 to nearly 23 per cent by 2036, while Uttar Pradesh moves from roughly 7 per cent to 12 per cent. Southern states, Himachal Pradesh and Punjab already have above-average elderly proportions, creating pockets where ageing, and the risk of isolation, are much more intense than the national average.The profile is also sharply gendered.Using LASI data, government summaries note a sex ratio of roughly 1,065 females per 1,000 males among India’s elderly, with women making up about 58 per cent of older persons. More than half of elderly women are widows. UNFPA’s ageing reports underline that older women are more likely to be widowed, have little personal income and live alone, increasing their vulnerability to economic insecurity and social isolation.This is the demographic stage on which India's loneliness economy is emerging.For generations, the family was India's first social safety net. That safety net has not disappeared, but it has changed shape.Migration, urbanisation, nuclear families and precarious work have moved millions away from the people and communities that once filled everyday life. A home that once had several generations may now have one person, one phone and a delivery app.The coping mechanisms are increasingly mediated by markets. Food delivery replaces the shared meal. OTT platforms fill the evening. Social media offers constant contact without necessarily providing companionship.For younger Indians, these services are conveniences. For an ageing population, they are becoming part of a much larger ecosystem of care.Policy has increasingly framed this demographic transition as an economic opportunity. NITI Aayog’s report Senior Care Reforms in India and the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment’s schemes speak of a “silver economy” built around the needs of older adults.Government estimates linked to the Senior Care Ageing Growth Engine (SAGE) initiative put the value of India’s silver economy at around Rs 73,000 crore in 2024, with scope for multi-fold expansion as the elderly population grows.The first pillar is financial security.Atal Pension Yojana subscriptions rose from about 1.54 crore in March 2019 to 8.27 crore by October 2025, according to Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority data quoted in government briefs. Assets under management crossed Rs 49,000 crore.The Indira Gandhi National Old Age Pension Scheme reaches around 2.21 crore beneficiaries, offering Rs 200 a month to those aged 60–79 and Rs 500 to those 80 and above. The amounts are modest, but they establish an income floor that can help with food, healthcare and basic services.The second pillar is health protection.Ayushman Bharat-PMJAY offers Rs 5 lakh a year in hospitalisation cover to vulnerable households. In October 2024, the government announced free treatment benefits of up to Rs 5 lakh annually for about 6 crore senior citizens aged 70 and above from 4.5 crore families, irrespective of socio-economic status. More than 40 lakh seniors had enrolled by January 2025.The National Programme for Health Care of the Elderly, meanwhile, now covers all 713 health districts, with geriatric OPDs, wards and physiotherapy services, according to the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.The third pillar is care.The SAGE portal invites start-ups to develop solutions in healthcare, housing, technology and social engagement, offering equity support of up to Rs 1 crore per project, with government equity capped at 49 per cent. The Integrated Programme for Senior Citizens grants fund 696 senior citizen homes across 29 States and UTs, with 84 more selected for 2025–26.A geriatric caregiver training scheme, run through 32 empanelled institutes, trained 36,785 caregivers in 2023–24.None of these programmes is explicitly called a loneliness programme. Yet they address many of the conditions that make loneliness more likely: widowhood, frailty, financial insecurity and living alone.Technology is becoming another layer of this support system.The government's e-Sanjeevani telemedicine platform has delivered nearly 14 crore tele-consultations nationwide, including more than 1 crore consultations involving senior citizens, according to a 2023 Press Information Bureau update and a national telemedicine assessment.For an older person who cannot easily travel, a consultation through a screen can be a lifeline. But the broader shift is significant too: an increasing number of needs, including emotional ones, are now being addressed through digital interfaces.Elderline 14567 offers senior citizens a telephone channel for grievances, abuse and emotional support, connecting callers to local services and authorities.The legal framework, meanwhile, continues to place responsibility on families. The Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007, and its 2019 amendment bill expand the definition of “children” and “parents”, remove the Rs 10,000 cap on maintenance, mandate that seniors should live a “life of dignity” and require dedicated police units and geriatric facilities in hospitals.The amendment widens “maintenance” to include healthcare, safety and housing. It is an acknowledgement that neglect, isolation and abuse are not merely private tragedies.But loneliness is not always about being physically alone.Anandeshwar, a civil services aspirant from Uttar Pradesh now living in Delhi, describes it as “not being understood or connected to the people around me, even when I am surrounded by others.”He has “a big enough circle of friends” but meets them rarely. Deeper conversations happen “pine ke baad” (after drinking). His ideal weekend is full: “write some good things, spend time with friends, participate in debating events”. Reality is often different. He stays home, “watch YT, Twitter, or just lay on the bed smoking,” joking that “every day is weakened for an unemployed.”The problem, then, is not simply the absence of people. It is the absence of meaningful connection.Loneliness often reveals itself through small choices.Cook or order in? Call someone or scroll? Go out or stay in bed?Minal divides her weekend between eating out and cooking at home. Neha's ideal weekend is “reading, writing and cleaning the flat”, even if reality is often errands and rest.Yukta, a psychology student from Bengaluru living in Jaipur, describes a more connected version of the same urban life. When she is not overwhelmed by work, weekends with her roommate mean “sleep, cooking and eating good food together, watching something fun on an OTT platform, or… a movie and cafe date! And shopping or just strolling through a bazaar for fun.”“Now, it feels like ‘I am finally home’,” she says. “I can do whatever I’d like, be whatever I’d like and just exist in a space with only comfort to offer.”LASI-based studies identify regular social contact, leisure activities, yoga or meditation and even moderate television or computer use as factors associated with better wellbeing among older adults.The difference is that in cities such as Delhi, Pune, Hyderabad and Jaipur, these activities are increasingly curated through markets: OTT subscriptions, cafes, delivery services and paid experiences.At the other end is Srabastee from West Bengal, living in Delhi for work.“It’s feeling like no one really knows what’s going on in your head, even when you’re surrounded by people… when you have something exciting or heartbreaking to share and can’t think of anyone to call.”She speaks to family and “texts a few friends once in a while”, mostly sharing memes and reacting to stories. Weeks can pass before they meet.After scrolling, she often feels “drained”, comparing her routine weekends of laundry, shows and ordered food with peers who seem to be “getting promoted, buying homes, getting married, travelling the world.”The same platforms that offer entertainment and convenience also fuel comparison. The same screens that connect people can deepen the feeling of being left behind.NITI Aayog argues that senior care needs to be recognised as a distinct sector, with regulations, standards, public-private partnerships and dedicated financing. Its vision combines public programmes such as pensions, NPHCE, telehealth and senior homes with private start-ups and NGOs.That is where the silver economy begins to overlap with the loneliness economy.The forces making Minal, Neha and Srabastee feel alone, migration, nuclear families, precarious jobs and digital overload, are also creating demand for therapy platforms, senior-living communities, companion-care services and digital communities.Some of these services may offer genuine support. Others may simply monetise a need that was once met by family, neighbours and community.The question becomes sharper when viewed against India's place near the top of the global loneliness index. A country ranked number two by JB.com is also preparing for a dramatic rise in its elderly population. By 2036, roughly 230 million Indians will be 60 or older. By 2050, nearly one in five will be.The challenge is not only how to feed, house or medically care for this population. It is also how to ensure that people have someone to talk to, someone who notices when they do not come home, someone who asks how their day was.India's loneliness economy is already taking shape, from pensions and senior homes to helplines, telehealth, therapy platforms, cafes, OTT subscriptions and delivery apps.The question is whether connection will ultimately be treated as a public responsibility or sold as another subscription.Because the people who today spend their Sundays between grocery stores and glowing screens will one day grow old. And the way India responds to their loneliness will reveal whether it sees the need to be seen, heard and remembered as a basic human need, or simply another market waiting to be tapped.
Article Summary: India is the second-loneliest country in the world, according to a study by jb.com. The ranking offers a stark lens through which to view a country that is aging rapidly. In 2011, india had about 100 million people aged 60 and above. Projections show that this figure will more than double to roughly 230 million by 2036.

Article Title: Can't force parents to create Apaar IDs for students: Supreme Court
Article Text: New Delhi: In an order that would apply to 24 crore students, Supreme Court on Monday said parents cannot be mandated to create 'one student, one unique ID' for their children under Automated Permanent Academic Account Registry (Apaar) scheme, even as it noted the benefits of the scheme.Appearing for three parents, whose children are studying in various grades in schools, senior advocate Indira Jaising said for creation of Apaar ID, schools are mandating the parents to provide Aadhaar number of their children, thus obliging them to create Aadhaar IDs for their children, even though SC in K S Puttaswamy case had said it would be optional. Moreover, the data pertaining to children stored through Apaar would endure through their lives. "No one, in their mid-careers, would want the public to know how they fared academically in their school days. It is purely a question of right to privacy ," she said and claimed that Digital Personal Data Protection Act provisions have not been applied to Apaar scheme.In addition, right to education is a fundamental right and not a service provided by govt. "For providing a service, the SC's 9-J bench had ruled that Aadhaar cannot be insisted upon for establishing one's identity," she said.She pointed out that Orissa high court had recently taken exception to parents being asked for consent for Apaar as it did not provide an 'opt out' option to parents and had directed state govt to consider amending the consent forms and providing such an option.The CJI-led bench asked whether the HC judgment had been challenged before SC. Jaising replied in the negative and said it has attained finality. SC said it would refer to the HC order and pass a ruling along similar lines which were enforceable pan-India.Before conveying its intended decision, the bench said everything need not be seen through the lens of suspicion. Creation of Apaar ID would help students shift from one school to another as transmission of their details can happen instantaneously, a task which used to take months with the tedious process of obtaining transfer certificates followed by physical transfer of records.The petitioners said, "The architecture of Apaar, involving centralised storage and updating of students' personal and academic data, effectively operates as a State-run surveillance infrastructure in the education sector."
Article Summary: Supreme court says parents cannot be mandated to create 'one student, one unique id' for their children under automated permanent academic account registry (apaar) scheme. Appearing for three parents, senior advocate indira jaising said for creation of apaar id, schools are mandating the parents to provide aadhaar number of their children.

Article Title: Rahul Gandhi claims PM Modi ‘most anti-youth PM India has seen’
Article Text:
Opposition slams the crackdown on young protestors

Rahul Gandhi

Shashi Tharoor

Akhilesh Yadav

Dimple Yadav

NEW DELHI: Congress leader Rahul Gandhi slammed PM Narendra Modi as the “most anti-youth PM India has ever had”, as the opposition jointly condemned the ruling BJP for the crackdown on “paper leak” protest march to Parliament Monday.Rahul said the young protesters are not criminals and Modi should accept their legitimate demands. “It is disgusting what the govt and the police are doing. Teargassing and lathicharging the students is not India’s way … all they believe in is ‘asatya’ and ‘hinsa’ which are anti-Indian principles,” he said in a video statement, adding, “Modi disrespects all the young people of this country.” He also said there is general outrage against RSS having captured the education system.After crackdown on young protestors, Rahul warned police officers that they will be held accountable. “Peaceful protest is a sacred and fundamental right. Every police officer and govt official blindly following orders to attack India’s innocent students should keep in mind - The Constitution is your master,” he said.Opposition’s Mallikarjun Kharge, Akhilesh Yadav (SP), Mamata Banerjee (TMC), Arvind Kejriwal (AAP), Dipankar Bhattacharya (CPI-ML), K C Venugopal, Priyanka Gandhi Vadra, Shashi Tharoor (Congress), N K Premachandran (RSP), D Raja (CPI), CPI (M), lashed out at the Modi govt for police action.Congress MP Renuka Chowdhury, CPI (M) general secretary M A Baby, SP MPs Dimple Yadav, Dharmendra Yadav, Iqra Hasan, Zia Ur Rahman Barq, Pushpendra Saroj, Chhote Lal, Rajiv Rai among others joined the protest near Parliament and Jantar Mantar.The full-blown attack came after anti-BJP parties hammered out a consensus on supporting the NEET protest on the opening day of the monsoon session of Parliament. In the meeting of the opposition parties Monday morning, sources said Congress and SP pushed for “Ayodhya donation scam” as the opening salvo against the ruling BJP. A senior leader is also said to have called the Cockroach Janata Party an RSS platform. But, sources said, other parties argued that they had to associate themselves with the youth march to Parliament.With the state action against students coupled with viral visuals dominating the headlines, Shiv Sena’s Sanjay Raut said the “paper leak issue” and “Ayodhya donation scam” will dominate Parliament in the coming days – in what seems an ominous sign for the initial days of proceedings in Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.Tharoor said the govt should allow some discussions being demanded by the opposition, and the latter will allow the bills to be taken up. “There has got to be give and take. That is the way Parliament is supposed to function,” he said.Akhilesh Yadav said the large youth turnout proves the failure of the BJP regime in conducting NEET and other examinations.Priyanka Gandhi, rebuking the govt for police action, said, “It’s ridiculous...They are our own children... they are the future of the country. This Parliament belongs to them. It’s no one’s personal fiefdom.”Dimple Yadav, who met the protesters, said, “This will be remembered as a black day. The youngsters came unarmed but faced lathis, were given electric shocks (taser). We saw bleeding heads.”DMK MP Kanimozhi dubbed protest as the most democratic form of expressing the people’s opinion. “There have been valid questions. Unfortunately, the govt does not want to answer the questions, and resorted to violence against the people raising the questions. This is unacceptable.”
Article Summary: Opposition slams the crackdown on young protestors. Rahul gandhi slammed pm narendra modi as the “most anti-youth pm india has ever had’ opposition parties hammered out a consensus on supporting the neet protest on the opening day of the monsoon session of parliament.

Article Title: AIIMS RDA seeks President’s intervention over Jantar Mantar crackdown, Wangchuk hospitalisation
Article Text: NEW DELHI: The Resident Doctors’ Association (RDA) of AIIMS, New Delhi, on Monday wrote to President Droupadi Murmu seeking an independent, impartial and time-bound inquiry into the events at Jantar Mantar, expressing concern over the alleged use of force against peaceful protesters and raising questions over the continued hospitalisation of climate activist Sonam Wangchuk.In its letter, the RDA said reports and widely circulated videos of the protest indicated the use of lathi charge, tear gas and physical force against protesters, including students, doctors and young citizens. While noting that certain aspects of the incident remain contested, the association said the available videos and eyewitness accounts had raised serious concerns over the manner in which peaceful democratic dissent was handled.The doctors’ body also expressed concern over videos purportedly showing police personnel with their faces and name badges covered, saying such actions undermine transparency and accountability. It further referred to videos allegedly showing security personnel threatening a professor and students of Lady Hardinge Medical College, saying the allegations warrant an impartial and transparent investigation.The association said the Constitution guarantees citizens the right to peaceful assembly and freedom of expression, adding that any response to peaceful protests must be guided by restraint, proportionality, accountability and respect for human dignity. If excessive force is established, it warned, it could erode public trust in democratic institutions.The RDA also flagged Wangchuk’s continued hospitalisation, saying reports suggesting he was not allowed to leave the hospital despite expressing a desire to take leave against medical advice (LAMA), if accurate, raise important questions about patient autonomy. It emphasised that informed consent and a competent patient’s right to refuse or discontinue treatment are fundamental ethical and legal principles of medical practice.The association urged the President to order an independent inquiry into the Jantar Mantar incident, ensure investigation into allegations of excessive force by law enforcement personnel, reaffirm citizens’ constitutional rights to peaceful protest, and seek clarification regarding the circumstances of Wangchuk’s continued hospitalisation while ensuring that patient rights and established principles of medical ethics are upheld.Describing itself as an apolitical organisation, the AIIMS RDA said it remained independent of political affiliations and believed that every individual has the democratic right to peaceful protest. It said preserving constitutional values is essential for maintaining public confidence in democratic and healthcare institutions.
Article Summary: The resident doctors’ association (rda) of aiims, new delhi, wrote to president droupadi murmu seeking an independent, impartial and time-bound inquiry into the events at jantar mantar. The rda said reports and widely circulated videos of the protest indicated the use of lathi charge, tear gas and physical force against protesters.

Article Title: Delhi violence not spontaneous, says BJP, blames Left
Article Text: NEW DELHI: BJP Monday blamed Left-affiliated student organisations, such as All India Students’ Association (AISA) and Students’ Federation of India (SFI), besides organised groups for protests and violence during Cockroach Janata Party’s march to Parliament.Similar allegations of organised instigation and crowd mobilisation were made in connection with the Delhi riots, and several individuals continue to face legal proceedings in those cases, BJP said. The violence did not appear spontaneous and there were organised groups that initiated it, after which sections of the crowd followed, it alleged.In a hard-hitting statement, BJP functionary Amit Malviya said that the “stone-pelting and orchestrated violence” carried out in the name of students raise serious questions about the intent of the protestors and those backing them.“Even more telling was the conspicuous absence of many of the prominent faces associated with the protest from the march itself, leaving those who had been mobilised at the behest of their political patrons to face the consequences,” he asked. The investigation will establish criminal responsibility, Malviya said.“The real conspirators are often those who plan, encourage and coordinate such violence while carefully keeping themselves out of harm’s way. They move on to the next agitation, leaving those they mobilised to bear the legal consequences.”CJP had been holding protest at Jantar Mantar since last month and has drawn support from opposition parties over its demand for the resignation of Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan for paper leaks.
Article Summary: Cjp had been holding protest at jantar mantar since last month. It has drawn support from opposition parties over its demand for the resignation of union education minister dharmendra pradhan for paper leaks. Bjp functionary amit malviya said that the “stone-pelting and orchestrated violence’ carried out in the name of students raise serious questions about the intent of the protestors.
